coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively studied in tumor therapy due to their excellent compatibility, remarkable stability, and luminescent properties. However, CDs-based therapeutic agents often face limitations such as rapid clearance, poor targeting, and weak responsiveness, which hinder their efficacy in achieving precise tumor treatment. To address these challenges, this study presents the construction of a dual-targeting, dual-responsive drug delivery platform with hierarchical structures: MV-DNA-CDs@DOX. Firstly, methacrylic acid-coated o-phenylenediamine carbon dots were synthesized using methacrylic acid and o-phenylenediamine. This was then conjugated with an i-motif-containing DNA sequence to create DNA-CDs with a “yarn ball-like” secondary structure. The nano-particles preserved the acid-responsive nature of the sequence and introduced an additional photo-responsive characteristic, enhancing the nanocarrier’s ability to deliver drugs deeply into tumor tissues. Upon loading with DOX, the polymeric nanocarrier was subject to biomimetic treatment, endowing it with active tumor-targeting capabilities and improving the specific distribution of the drug within tumor sites. Therefore, the use of biocompatible and responsive DNA segments as crosslinkers to construct organic nanopolymer carriers in the preparation of anti-tumor nanomaterials holds promise in expanding the application of nanotechnology in the field of cancer treatment.

head.ce:title: DNA-based nanocomposites with secondary structure for precision treatment of tumor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using responsive DNA i-motif segments as crosslinkers, a biomimetic organic nanopolymer carrier exhibits dual responsiveness and targeting capabilities enabling improved delivery of drugs to deep-seated tumors for precision cancer therapy.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively studied in tumor therapy due to their excellent compatibility, remarkable stability, and luminescent properties. However, CDs-based therapeutic agents often face limitations such as rapid clearance, poor targeting, and weak responsiveness, which hinder their efficacy in achieving precise tumor treatment. To address these challenges, this study presents the construction of a dual-targeting, dual-responsive drug delivery platform with hierarchical structures: MV-DNA-CDs@DOX. Firstly, methacrylic acid-coated o-phenylenediamine carbon dots were synthesized using methacrylic acid and o-phenylenediamine. This was then conjugated with an i-motif-containing DNA sequence to create DNA-CDs with a “yarn ball-like” secondary structure. The nano-particles preserved the acid-responsive nature of the sequence and introduced an additional photo-responsive characteristic, enhancing the nanocarrier’s ability to deliver drugs deeply into tumor tissues. Upon loading with DOX, the polymeric nanocarrier was subject to biomimetic treatment, endowing it with active tumor-targeting capabilities and improving the specific distribution of the drug within tumor sites. Therefore, the use of biocompatible and responsive DNA segments as crosslinkers to construct organic nanopolymer carriers in the preparation of anti-tumor nanomaterials holds promise in expanding the application of nanotechnology in the field of cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots, are a novel type of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial [1]. Given their unique optical properties, outstanding biocompatibility, and ease of production [2], carbon dots have found extensive applications in the biomedical field, including bioimaging, drug delivery, and disease diagnosis. In particularly, in the field of cancer diagnosis and treatment [3], the exciting-depended emission fluorescence of CDs have been wildly used for multi-level discriminability bioimaging of cancer/normal cells or tissues [4], even for subcellular organelles localization microimaging [5]. Meanwhile, the imaging assisted drug delivery platform (IADDP) derived from CDs, achieving accurately tracking the drug delivery in vivo, resulting for visualization the ADME behavior (absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion) of drug-carriers, and IADDP constructing also provided referenceable data for precise tumor delivery of radio/chemotherapy agents [6].

Accompanied by in-depth research, CDs-based IADDP for cancer theragnostics have gradually exposed imperfections, which seriously restrict the delivery behavior in vivo of IADDP, especially for spatiotemporal tumor targeting and the release behavior of therapeutic agents [7], etc. In generally, the obstacles are mainly embodied in two aspects, namely poor size regulation and ignored fluorescence operation. Specifically, CDs usually dispersed independently with uniformly small-sized (< 5 nm) spherical morphology [8], which enabled to circulate with the blood and quickly be cleared through the kidneys [9]. But the always entities ordinarily ambition have both sides, and while smaller diameter of CDs provided the outstanding internal distribution and clearance efficiency, that also makes it a less maintain the effective detention time and concentration for disease treatment, especially, that not conducive to tumor targeting drug enrichment relying on EPR effect (∼ 50–200 nm is preferred) [10]. Another malpractice aspect (or not turn material resources to good account) is the neglecting the versatility and insufficient application of fluorescence. In other words, previous studies on CDs bio-applications have mostly focused on bio-imaging [11], while disregarding the additional value of fluorescence for proactive applications, such as photoluminescence (PL)-triggered drug release [12]. PL-triggered release is beneficial for achieving targeted drug release selectivity and reducing drug leakage caused by passive release mechanisms, such as pH-sensitive indiscriminate drug release in the tumor’s acidic microenvironment [13], thereby improving biosafety.

In order to endow CDs-based IADDP with considerable tumor targeting and controllable drug release, traditional CDs have been improved into CDs-complexes with special sizes or surface ligand structures via either simple physical regulation (charge/hydrophobic interactions) or chemical modification, or a combination of both methods [14]. Through comprehensive comparison and stability verification, chemical modification, that is introducing surface ligands or sensitive chemical bonds by chemical reaction, could better achieve precise morphology control and sensitive drug release of the artificial CDs-complexes compared to physical methods [15]. Specifically, modified by surface ligands possessing with special chemical properties, such as amphiphilicity, crosslink-ability, or even the propensity for click reaction, CDs could be further self-assembled with the expected morphology and structure [16]; and exhibited long-term circulation and better EPR effect in tumor treatment. In addition, the as-prepared CDs could further couple with active targeted ligands or antibodies on the surface through chemical reactions for increasing the aggregation concentration and penetration depth of CDs-composites at the tumor site. In terms of fluorescence initiative of CDs-based materials constructed artificially, researchers have achieved light triggered targeted drug release by modifying sensitive photodynamic bonds, such as Ru-Se can be cleaved under visible light irradiation [17], which completed the operability of drug release. In other words, the subjectively controllable drug release prevents the premature leakage and undifferentiated release in traditional passive tumor microenvironment (acidic environment or high concentration of glutathione) sensitive drug release. Meanwhile, researchers even achieved sensitive targeted drug release through the photothermal properties of carbon dots, and exhibited good therapeutic effects [18].

Further analysing the curative effect, such as targeted localization and behavior in vivo, of the size regulation and drug sensitive release of CDs-based nanocomplexes by means of chemical modification, it was found that constrained by the small-sized primary spherical structure, CDs has fewer surface functional groups available for chemical reactions [19], and could not achieve size controlled, sensitive bond introduction and targeted ligand modification, simultaneously. Meanwhile, the quenching of fluorescence is extremely easy caused by complex and multi-step chemical reaction regulation on the surface of the as-prepared CDs. On the contrary, if the primary structure is regulated during the synthesis process of initial carbon dots, that is, changing the small size or solid spherical structure of CDs to leave more modifiable groups [20], it will be conducive to further sensitive bond introduction and surface modification, and more conducive to the maintenance of fluorescence. In addition, we also found that the traditional surface modification with small molecule or polymer could not fundamentally change the primary structure of CDs to achieve precise artificial regulation of nanoparticles. Inspired by the multi-level regulation of biological macromolecules (such as the quaternary structure of protein and the double helix structure of DNA) [21], introducing the concept of secondary/multilevel structure into the surface regulation of CDs will facilitate the realization of multilevel morphology and sensitive drug release regulation according to artificial expected, and change the biological characteristic behavior. DNA was able to achieve the specific shape regulation precisely through the unique principle of complementary base pairing [22]. Research has shown that compared to traditional chemical modification methods, nanoparticle with DNA single stranded on the surface could achieve more precise and desired regulation of the particle size/morphology through DNA specific base complementary pairing [23]. Meanwhile, the double-stranded DNA with a special base sequence could collapse sensitively for external environment (light or pH) actively or passively, further causing the disintegration of CDs-based assemblies artificially controllable [24]. Therefore, compared to existing drug delivery platforms, we are more focused on multi-level regulation of CDs. By DNA modification, we have achieved controlled release and targeted regulation of drugs, aiming to fill the research gaps in the drug carrier field, which are caused by the shortcomings such as the small particle size of CDs.

This study aims to design a biomimetic nanopolymeric carrier with secondary structure, which features dual tumor targeting and dual responsiveness. This carrier is a DNA-conjugated carbon dot composite that encapsulates the chemotherapeutic model drug Doxorubicin (DOX) for precise cancer treatment. As Scheme 1 , we synthesized methacrylic acid-coated o-phenylenediamine carbon dots (primary structure) through a one-pot method using methacrylic acid and o-phenylenediamine. Subsequently, these carbon dots were further conjugated with a DNA sequence containing i-motif elements to construct a “yarn-like” secondary structure, forming the polymeric nanocarrier DNA-CDs. This assembly retained the acid responsiveness of the i-motif sequences [25] and acquired an additional light-responsive characteristic. After loading DOX, the nanoparticle was subjected to biomimetic treatment to enhance the specific distribution of the drug at tumor sites, resulting in the final therapeutic nanomaterial, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX. We validated the successful synthesis of the nanomaterials using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), agarose gel electrophoresis, and SDS-PAGE techniques. Subsequent in vitro and in vivo experiments were conducted to examine the responsiveness, permeability, and targeting abilities of the nanomaterials, and to evaluate their effectiveness in precise tumor treatment. In conclusion, we utilized biocompatible responsive DNA fragments containing the i-motif as crosslinking agents to construct biomimetic organic nanopolymeric carriers. This approach, which aims to achieve enhanced permeability and tumor targeting for anticancer applications, holds significant implications for cancer treatment and advances the study of carbon dots as drug carriers.

Di-tert-butyl hydrogen peroxide (98 %) and methacrylic acid (99 %), was obtained from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine and methanol were purchased from Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), was obtained from RHAWN (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and agarose were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was obtained from Sango Biotech (Shanghai, China). Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl) and indocyanine green (ICG) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The Cytochalasin B (CB) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). TE buffer (1X) and DNA were obtained from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium Nutrient Mixture F-12 (Ham) (DMEM/F-12) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, USA). Certified fetal bovine serum (FBS) was STR-authenticated by Shanghai XP Biomed Ltd (Viva Cell, Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used in this work was produced by an ultra-pure-water purification system from Research Water Purification Technology Co., Ltd (Xiamen, China).

Murine macrophage cell lines (RAW 264.7) and murine breast cancer 4 T1 cell lines (4 T1) were obtained from the Cell Resource Center, Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA).

Healthy female BALB/c mice (15–––20 g) was purchased from Laboratory Animal Center, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, China. All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Chongqing Medical University and the experiments were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

Ultraviolete-visible (UV–vis) absorption was collected on Model Ultra-6600A (Rigol, China). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by an RF5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by the Nicolet iS5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscopy (TEM, America) was used for morphological images of chiral CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Intracellular fluorescence was obtained on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica, Germany). MTT assay results were analyzed using a Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Imaging of visceral organs and in vivo imaging of CDs were studied using VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea). The tissue sections, H&E staining and evaluation of liver and kidney function were obtained from Chengdu Scientist Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MAA CDs, ssDNA-CDs and DNA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MAA CDs: MAA CDs were synthesized by methacrylic acid (MAA), Di-tert-butyl hydrogen peroxide (DTBP), and o-phenylenediamine as main raw materials. 0.025 g of o-phenylenediamine, 500 μL of methacrylic acid, and 63.2 μL of DTBP were mixed in an autoclave. Adjust the pH to 7.4 and dilute with ultrapure water to a final volume of 10 mL. Then, the autoclave was transferred to a muffle furnace and heated to 180 °C for 8 h. After reaction, MAA CDs was synthesized. The products were purified by dialysis bag for subsequent experiments.

ssDNA-CDs: 7 mg EDC was added to 1.4 mL MAA CDs solution to activate for 15 min, then, 700 μL mixed CDs solution was transferred to 175 μL ssDNA1 (or ssDNA2). Followed by the addition of 1.75 mg NHS, and the reaction was carried out for 2 h. After that, the above mixture was placed in 4℃ overnight to synthesize ssDNA1-CDs and ssDNA2-CDs, respectively. The i-motif DNA sequences are ssDNA1: CTG TCA TCG GTC ACA CAT TCC TAA GTC TGA CCC TAA CCC TAA CCC TAA CCC; ssDNA2: GAC AGT AGC CAG TGT GTA AGG ATT CAG ACT GGG ATT GGG ATT GGG ATT GGG.

DNA-CDs: Then, ssDNA1-CDs and ssDNA2-CDs (V1:V2 = 1:1) were heated in a water bath at 95 ℃ for 5 min, respectively, and mixed thoroughly. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the mixture was transferred in the condition overnight to synthesize DNA-CDs.

4 T1 cells was cultured in DMEM/F-12 medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and streptomycin/penicillin and incubated at 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. HUVEC cells and RAW 264.7 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and streptomycin/penicillin and incubated at 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

MTT assay was conducted on HUVEC cells and 4 T1 cells to evaluate cytotoxicity of DNA-CDs at the short time (24 h) and long time (48 h) in vitro. HUVEC cells and 4 T1 cells were added into 96-well plates at the density of 5 × 103 cell per well, respectively, and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the medium in each well was replaced by a working medium containing a range of concentration of DNA-CDs (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μg/mL). After incubated for 24 h or 48 h, the medium was removed and replaced with fresh DMEM/F-12 medium containing MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 10 μL) for another 4 h. Furthermore, DMSO was used to replace medium and incubate for 4 h. Cell viability was calculated by the absorbance value at 450 nm, which was detected by using a microplate reader (Varioskan LUX).

Blood of BALB/c mice were collected from posterior orbital vein and centrifuged immediately in 3000 rpm/min for 10 min to separate the precipitate obtaining red blood cells. Collected red blood cells were wash by PBS for four times and resuspend with PBS to prepare 5 % suspension of red blood cells. Then, 1 mL suspension was added to 1 mL PBS as a negative group, and to 1 mL purified water as a positive group. 1 mL suspension was added to 800 μL PBS and 200 μL different DNA-CDs solution (10, 200, 500 and 1000 μg/mL). The mixed suspensions were incubated for 12 h and centrifuged at 1000 rpm/min for 10 min. Finally, the tubes were carefully removed from the centrifuge, photographed and measured the absorbance of supernatant. The hemolysis was calculated by the following formula.

To load DOX into DNA-CDs, 2 mg DOX⋅HCl was added to 1 mL DNA-CDs solution under dark condition and at room temperature with stirring for 24 h. Furthermore, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm/min for 10 min, and the participate was collected and washed by purified water to obtain purified DNA-CDs@DOX.

In order to determine the responsive drug release of DNA-CDs@DOX at different conditions, the standard curves of model drug DOX were first measured. The absorption of different concentrate of DOX were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The standard curve was obtained according to the fluorescence luminosity value and concentration.

To determine the responsive release of drugs in DNA-CDs@DOX under different pH conditions, the DNA-CDs@DOX solution was placed into dialysis bags and transferred into 30 mL of PBS with varying conditions (pH 7.4, 6.5, and 5.5). The samples were slowly stirred at 37 ℃ and 300 rpm/min to facilitate drug release. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of the release medium was collected and transferred to corresponding vials, followed by adding 1 mL of PBS to the vials. Finally, all samples were measured using fluorescence spectrophotometry, and the concentration of DOX was quantified by correlating the fluorescence intensity with a standard curve.

To determine the responsive release of drugs in DNA-CDs@DOX under UV light conditions, DNA-CDs@DOX samples treated with UV irradiation (10 or 15 min) were placed into dialysis bags and transferred into 30 mL of pH 6.5 PBS. The samples were slowly stirred at 37 ℃, 300 rpm/min, and under 365 nm UV light irradiation (10 or 15 min) to facilitate drug release. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of the release medium was collected and transferred into corresponding vials, followed by adding 1 mL of PBS to the vials. Finally, all samples were measured using fluorescence spectrophotometry, and the concentration of DOX was quantified by correlating the fluorescence intensity with a standard curve.

RAW 264.7 cells were washed with two times and cultured in serum-free DMEM/F-12 containing 10 μg/mL CB for 1 h. Then, tread RAW 264.7 cells were transferred into a centrifuge tube and vibrated ceaselessly for 5 min. An equal volume of FBS was added to the centrifuge tube and mixed evenly. The mixture was centrifuged at 1000 rpm/min for 15 min, then the supernatant was centrifuged at 4000 rpm/min for 15 min. After that, the participate was collected and washed with 0.25 % EDTA solution for two times (MV: macrophage-derived vesicles).
DNA-CDs@DOX was added to MV and mixed. The mixture was treated with ultrasound for 2 min, and stop for 1 min. After repeating five times, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX was obtained.

MV-DNA-CDs@DOX was centrifuged at 10000 rpm/min for 15 min, then the participate was collected for SDS-PAGE and WB analysis. All samples were separated by 12 % SDS-PAGE at 120 V; then, a piece of gel was stained with Coomassie brilliant blue and washed with water until the background is clean. Another piece of gel was used in WB.

The in vitro therapeutic efficacy of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX can be assessed by observing the morphological changes in 4 T1 tumor spheroids and by using the MTT assay to evaluate cell toxicity. Morphology observation experiment of 4 T1 tumor spheroid to evaluate cytotoxicity of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX. 4 T1 tumor spheroids were established by the hanging drop method according to previous reports. Suspension of 4 T1 tumor spheroid was added to a 24-plate, and photographed by a microscope. Then, PBS, DNA-CDs, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX were added to the 24-plate, respectively, and the final concentration of above materials is 100 μg/mL. After cultured for 24 h, morphology of 4 T1 tumor spheroids with different treated were photographed.

To further evaluate the cytotoxicity of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX, MTT assay was conducted on 4 T1 cells of two-dimension and three-dimensional tumor spheroids. 4 T1 cells were added into 96-well plates at the density of 5 × 103 cell per well, respectively, and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the medium in each well was removed, DNA-CDs, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX with a range of concentration (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL) were added into wells, respectively. And MV-DNA-CDs@DOX treated with UV irradiation (10,15 min) were added into wells, respectively. After incubated for 24 h, the medium was removed and replaced with fresh DMEM/F-12 medium containing MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 10 μL) for another 4 h. Furthermore, DMSO was used to replace medium and incubate for 4 h to dissolve formazan. Cell viability was calculated by the absorbance value at 450 nm, which was detected by using a microplate reader.

4 T1 tumor spheroids were added into 96-well plates, DNA-CDs, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX with a range of concentration (0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μg/mL) were added into wells, respectively. After cultured for 24 h, 20 μL of Alma blue reagent was added to each well. Then, the cells were incubated in the incubator for 2–6 h until the color of the medium changed from indigo blue to pink. Cell viability was calculated by fluorescence intensity (λex = 530 nm, λem = 590 nm).

4 T1 tumor spheroids were added into 96-well plates, and cultured with different treatments for 24 h. The specific grouping is DOX (pH 6.5); DNA-CDs@DOX (pH 6.5); DNA-CDs@DOX (pH 7.4); MV-DNA-CDs@DOX (pH 6.5); DNA-CDs@DOX (pH 6.5) + 365 nm UV irradiation. The final concentration of DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX is 50 μg/mL. Afterwards, the tumor spheres were transferred to dishes of confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM), and DOX fluorescence at different depths in 3D tumor spheres was observed by CLSM (Leica TCS-SP2).

The acid environment of lysosome is suitable for the responsive drug release of DNA-CDs@DOX, therefore the lysosome colocalization was determined. 4 T1 cells were added into dishes of CLSM at the density of 5 × 103 cell per well, respectively, and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the fresh culture medium containing DNA-CDs@DOX (100 μg/mL) were added into the dishes. After cultured for 24 h, cells were washed by PBS and cultured in per-warmed staining working solution (Lyso-Tracker Red) for 30 min. Then, the fresh culture medium containing DAPI replaced the staining working solution. After 15 min, lysosome colocalization was observed by CLSM.

To detect the tumor targeting and biodistribution in vivo, the near-infrared light dye with better tissue penetration was applied in the test. Therefore, ICG were loaded in DNA-CDs and MV-DNA-CDs to prepare DNA-CDs@ICG and MV-DNA-CDs@ICG, respectively. 100 μL of PBS containing 4 T1 cells (2 × 106 cell/mL) was injected into the BALB/c mouse to construct subcutaneous tumor model. After that, 200 μL of free ICG, DNA-CDs@ICG and MV-DNA-CDs@ICG (100 μg/kg) were injected to the tail vein of BALB/c mice. Then, at the predetermined time points (15 min, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 and 36 h), ICG fluorescence distribution in mice was observed by the invasive near infrared optical imaging system.

To furtherly investigate the tumor targeting and biodistribution in mice, ex vivo fluorescence of tumors and tissues were observed. 100 μL of PBS containing 4 T1 cells (2 × 106 cell/mL) was injected into the BALB/c mouse to construct subcutaneous tumor model. Then, 200 μL of free ICG, DNA-CDs@ICG and MV-DNA-CDs@ICG (100 μg/kg) were injected to the tail vein of BALB/c mice. After 4 h, the mice were sacrificed to collect tumors and the major organs, which were observed by the VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea).

100 μL of PBS containing 4 T1 cells (2 × 106 cell/mL) was injected into the BALB/c mouse to construct subcutaneous tumor model. After that, mice were divided to seven groups (n = 5/group): PBS, DNA-CDs, UV, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX + UV. Every 2 days, the weight of mice and volume of tumor were measured by the electronic scale and vernier caliper. After that, 100 μL of PBS, DNA-CDs, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX (5 mg/kg DOX) were injected by tail vein. The volume of tumor was calculated by the formula: Volume = (Length * Width2)/2. After 14 days of therapy, the mice were sacrificed to collect tumors and the major organs, the tumors were weighted to evaluate therapy effect.

After therapy, hearts, livers, spleens, lungs and kidneys of treated mice were collected and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde. Then, the major organs were embedded in paraffin and sliced for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and TUNEL staining. Histological analysis was performed by observing at images of thin sections obtained using an optical microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis and characterization of DNA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of DNA-conjugated carbon dots (DNA-CDs) began with a one-pot reaction using methylacrylic acid (MAA) and o-phenylenediamine, which produced the fluorescent MAA carbon dots (MAA CDs) as the primary structure. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) displayed MAA CDs with a bilayered structure, indicating that methylacrylic acid encapsulates the o-phenylenediamine carbon dots (oP-CDs) (Fig. 1 A). Red arrows in the insert of Fig. 1A highlight the oP-CDs in the bilayer structure. Analysis revealed an average size of 4.25 nm for the oP-CDs, and 31.33 nm for the complete MAA CDs (Fig. 1D and 1E). Furthermore, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) exhibited distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.39 nm (Fig. 1B). Subsequently, DNA sequences containing i-motif structures were conjugated to the MAA CDs to form the DNA-CDs nanocarriers. TEM characterization of the resulting polymer nanocarriers indicated a “yarn ball-like” morphology, with i-motif DNA sequences adorning the outer layer of the carbon dots (Fig. 1C). Size and zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Malvern particle size analyzer. The results indicated that the average hydrodynamic diameter of DNA-CDs was 270 nm (Fig. 1F), which is similar to the diameter observed from TEM. This discrepancy can be attributed to the hydrated state of particles measured by the Malvern instrument, as well as the presence of hydrophilic groups (amino and carboxyl groups) on the DNA-CDs that form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, thereby increasing the measured size. Zeta potential measurements were employed to assess the stability of the MAA CDs and DNA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1G, the average zeta potential of the MAA CDs was −7.38 (± 0.19) mV, whereas the potential decreased to −16.97 (± 0.50) mV upon conjugation with the DNA sequences. This demonstrates that DNA-CDs exhibit enhanced stability compared to unmodified MAA CDs.

Fluorescence is the most prominent and widely used feature of CDs. The inserted pictures in Fig. 1H and 1I are photographs of MAA CDs and DNA-CDs under white light and UV light. Under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, MAA CDs and DNA-CDs exhibit similar yellow fluorescence. To determine the fluorescence properties, a fluorescence spectrophotometer was used. And the fluorescence spectrophotometer further confirmed that both MAA CDs and DNA-CDs have similar fluorescent properties, indicating that DNA-CDs possess excellent fluorescence properties. However, the fluorescence intensity of MAA CDs is higher, which may be attributed to the DNA-CDs synthesis process requiring heating in a water bath at 95 °C, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence of carbon dots (Fig. 1H and 1I). Using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) to measure the fluorescence lifetimes of MAA CDs and DNA-CDs, the results further indicate that MAA CDs and DNA-CDs exhibit similar fluorescence properties, suggesting that the attachment of DNA sequences does not affect the fluorescence of the carbon dots (Fig. S1).

The illustration of Fig. 2 A shows the MAA CDs and DNA-CDs captured under white light. And the synthesized MAA CDs and DNA-CDs solutions were both pale yellow in color. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy revealed a significant increase in the DNA characteristic absorption peak at 260 nm for DNA-CDs compared to MAA CDs alone, providing preliminary evidence for the successful binding of the DNA sequence to the carbon dots (Fig. 2A). In order to determine the surface structure of the MAA CDs, FTIR spectra were obtained in the range of 500 to 4000 cm−1. The results showed stretching vibration peaks at 1410 cm−1, 1540 cm−1, 1700 cm−1, and 3430 cm−1, corresponding to C-N, C = N, C = O, and O-H/N-H bonds, indicating the presence of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the MAA CDs. The appearance of C-N and C = N peaks suggested that amidation reactions occurred between the amino and carboxyl groups of the MAA CDs during the high-temperature polymerization process (Fig. 2B) [26].

XPS analysis was carried out to assess the elemental composition and valence states of DNA-CDs. Fig. 2C indicated that the DNA-CDs are composed of C, N, O, and P elements. Peak deconvolution of the C1s spectrum revealed peaks at 284.7 eV (C = C/C-C), 285.6 eV (C-O/C-N), and 287.8 eV (C = N/C = O), with the presence of C-N and C = N bonds in DNA-CDs. These peaks could be attributed to the formation of new bonds between CDs and DNA, or they could all be pre-existing bonds in the CDs (Fig. 2D) [27,28]. The O1s spectrum showed peaks at 531.1 eV (C = O) and 533.2 eV (C-O) (Fig. 2E). The N1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 399.8 eV (−N = ), 399.2 eV (–NH2), and 399.8 eV (–NH-) (Fig. 2F) [29]. The P2p spectrum showed peaks at 128.5 eV (P3/2) and 130.5 eV (P1/2), confirming the presence of P2-, with a peak at 134.3 eV corresponding to P-O bonds (Fig. 2G) [30]. To further confirm the successful coupling of DNA and carbon dots, agarose gel electrophoresis was performed. Fig. 2H and 2I showed that in lane 1, ssDNA migrated quickly and evenly, with a high band intensity. Lane 2 showed no band for MAA CDs. In lanes 3 and 4, the band intensity decreased and became wider, indicating that MAA CDs bound to DNA resulted in a decreased migration rate of DNA. In lane 5, the band became even wider, suggesting that the interaction between ssDNA-CDs was increased through complementary base pairing, further restricting DNA migration. The agarose gel blocks were placed under a 365 nm UV lamp to observe the fluorescence. The fluorescence color of lane 5 was between lane 3 and lane 4, indicating the successful connection of ssDNA1-CDs and ssDNA2-CDs. These results collectively confirm the successful connection of DNA and MAA CDs, resulting in the synthesis of a polymer nanomaterial, DNA-CDs, that is distinct from carbon dots.

CDs serve as photo-luminescent carriers, allowing for drug release monitoring and aiding in imaging-guided drug delivery. DOX is one of the most common anti-tumor drugs. The illustration in Fig. 3 A shows that after DNA-CDs encapsulates DOX, the solution changes from yellow to red under white light conditions. UV–Vis spectroscopy demonstrates that DNA-CDs@DOX exhibits characteristic absorption peaks at 440 nm (attributed to DNA-CDs) and 510 nm (attributed to DOX), indicating the successful encapsulation of DOX within DNA-CDs (Fig. 3A). Fluorescence spectroscopy is also utilized to characterize the encapsulation of DOX. The image inserted in Fig. 3B was taken under 365 nm ultraviolet light of DOX, DNA-CDs and DNA-CDs@DOX. And the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 3B shows that DNA-CDs@DOX exhibits emission peaks at 554 nm (DNA-CDs) and 586 nm (DOX) when excited at a wavelength of 480 nm. Under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, DNA-CDs emit yellow fluorescence, while DNA-CDs@DOX displays orange fluorescence, indicating a red-shift in fluorescence after the encapsulation of red-emitting DOX by DNA-CDs, further confirming the successful encapsulation of DOX.

Subsequently, we investigated the release characteristics of DNA-CDs@DOX. To evaluate the release of DOX from DNA-CDs@DOX at different pH values, we established UV–Visible and fluorescence standard curves for DOX using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively (Fig. S2). We then measured the fluorescence of DOX released from DNA-CDs@DOX at different time points.

In the initial design, an i-motif structure with acid-responsive properties was incorporated into the DNA-CDs structure. Dialysis was used to verify whether DNA-CDs could release DOX in response to pH changes. The release rate of DOX was calculated using the fluorescence intensity standard curve of DOX concentration. The pH-responsive drug release of DNA-CDs was examined under different pH conditions. Under pH 7.4 conditions, only 22.8 % of DOX was released from the carrier DNA-CDs. As the pH decreased to 6.5, the release rate of DOX increased to 44.7 %. At pH 5.5, the release rate of DOX further increased to 58.0 % (Fig. 3C). These results indicate that DNA-CDs can respond to the acidic tumor environment and release a significant amount of DOX. The responsive release characteristics reduce the systemic toxicity of DOX and increase its efficacy in tumor treatment. This is beneficial for DNA-CDs as a drug carrier for treating solid tumors, as the pH of solid tumors is lower than that of normal tissues.

Furthermore, the UV light-responsive property of DNA-CDs was also validated. Compared to the control group, the release rate of DOX significantly increased upon exposure to UV light. Specifically, after 10 min of irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, the release rate of DOX reached 46.5 %. Extending the irradiation time by an additional 15 min increased the drug release rate to 53.0 %, thereby further enhancing the release of DOX (Fig. 3D). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for further characterization to validate the responsive release property of DNA-CDs. Fig. 3E shows that DNA-CDs can be broken down into smaller particles after acid responsive treatment and UV light irradiation compared to the initial particles in Fig. 1C. These results demonstrate that the nano-carrier DNA-CDs possess a dual-responsive drug release property to both pH and UV light.

During the acidic dissolution process of DNA-CDs@DOX in response to the acidic environment, the pH value influences the dissolution efficacy. The results show that DNA-CDs@DOX has a more complete dissolution and drug release at pH 5.5. And pH 5.5 is a relatively harsh condition for biological tissues, with the pH value in the slightly acidic microenvironment of tumor cells being approximately 6.5. Only the acidity of lysosomes inside tumor cells can reach such a low pH. Therefore, it was examined whether the nano-materials could be localized in lysosomes. The merged image results show a clear overlap between the green fluorescence of DNA-CDs@DOX and the red fluorescence of lysosomes, indicating that DNA-CDs@DOX can be localized in lysosomes, which facilitates the release of DOX from DNA-CDs@DOX (Fig. 3F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and targeted Validation of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX
ce:section.ce:para: During the biomimetic construction of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX, macrophages, macrophage-derived microvesicles, and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX shared the same proteins. Therefore, SDS-PAGE gel electrophoresis and Western Blot were used to verify the success of the biomimetic process. Coomassie Brilliant Blue staining results showed that macrophages, macrophage-derived microvesicles, and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX exhibited similar protein expression, while the DNA-CDs@DOX group showed no protein signal. This indicates the successful extraction of macrophage-derived microvesicles and the incorporation of macrophages with the nanomaterial (Fig. 4 A). Moreover, Western Blot analysis revealed the presence of CD11b protein on the surfaces of macrophages, macrophage-derived microvesicles, and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX, further confirming the success of the biomimetic process (Fig. 4B). After staining MV-DNA-CDs@DOX with DIO green dye, green fluorescence from the membrane and red fluorescence from DOX could be observed under confocal microscopy. The merged image showed good fusion of the green and red fluorescence signals, indicating a good wrapping of the nanomaterial by the macrophage-derived microvesicles (Fig. 4C). The morphology of carbon dot nanomaterials was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, and the results showed that MV-DNA-CDs@DOX exhibited a spherical or quasi-spherical structure (Fig. S3A). Additionally, according to EDS elemental mapping, the signals for C, N, O, and P elements coexist in the EDS spectrum of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX (Fig. S3B-G), further indicating successful integration of macrophage-derived microvesicles with the nanomaterials. Fig. S4 and S5 demonstrate that the carrier exhibits good biocompatibility, indicating its suitability for delivering chemotherapy drugs for cancer treatment.

The cytotoxic effects of different materials on 4 T1 cells were determined using the MTT assay. Fig. 4D shows that treatment of 4 T1 cells with concentrations lower than 50 μg/mL resulted in increased cell toxicity with increasing DOX concentration, indicating the effective chemotherapeutic effect of DOX. However, the carrier DNA-CDs had minimal impact on cell viability. At a concentration of 50 μg/mL of DNA-CDs@DOX, cell toxicity reached 30.5 %; compared to DOX alone, cell toxicity increased by 26.4 %. This suggests a significant enhancement of cell toxicity after DOX delivery through the carrier. When 4 T1 cells were treated with a concentration of 50 μg/mL, the cell viability in the DNA-CDs@DOX group was significantly reduced, about 4.5 times more toxic than the DOX group. When the concentration of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX was 10 to 50 μg/mL, cell viability was lower than that of the DNA-CDs@DOX group, indicating that membrane encapsulation further improved the cell toxicity of DNA-CDs@DOX. DNA-CDs@DOX (50 μg/mL) combined with 365 nm UV irradiation for 10 min resulted in a cell viability of only 57.3 %, which was 8.7 % lower than the non-irradiated control group (Fig. 4E). Additionally, to study the tumor-targeted delivery of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX, intracellular drug release, and the process of inducing apoptosis, flow cytometry assays (Fig. S6), live/dead dual staining (Fig. S7A), and dynamic process experiments of DOX-induced changes in individual 4 T1 cells (Fig. S7B) were utilized. The results indicate that MV-DNA-CDs@DOX possesses tumor targeting properties, and DOX begins to enter the cell nucleus within 1 h (Fig. S8). Significant morphological changes in the cells occur after 6 h, and cell apoptosis is observed after 8 h. The experimental results are consistent throughout.

A hanging drop method was used to prepare 3D tumor spheroids to more accurately simulate the real tumor environment in vivo and further investigate the in vitro anti-tumor effects of different materials. Fig. 4F shows that the cell killing ability of each group was somewhat restricted in the tumor spheroid model. The cell toxicity of 20 μg/mL DOX was only 6.5 %, and even at a high concentration of DOX (500 μg/mL DOX), the cell toxicity was only 28.5 %. However, when the concentration of DNA-CDs@DOX exceeded 100 μg/mL, the 3D cell toxicity was comparable to the 2D cell toxicity. It is worth noting that within the concentration range of 0 to 500 μg/mL, the tumor cell toxicity of the MV-DNA-CDs@DOX group was higher than that of DNA-CDs@DOX, indicating that the encapsulated material had superior therapeutic effects in vivo.

To further illustrate its therapeutic effect, 3D tumor sphere morphology was observed to obtain a clearer understanding of the impact of different treatments on the spheres. The preparation method for 3D tumor spheres was the same as mentioned above, and the morphology of the spheres was observed and recorded under an optical microscope. The spheres were treated with PBS, DNA-CDs, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX for 24 h. After the treatment, the morphology of the tumor spheres was observed and recorded using an optical microscope. Fig. 4G shows that there was no significant change in the morphology of the tumor spheres before and after treatment in the PBS and DNA-CDs groups, indicating that DNA-CDs itself does not have a therapeutic effect on the tumor. Compared to the DOX group, the morphology of the tumor spheres in the DNA-CDs@DOX and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX groups showed more pronounced changes. From the perspective of tumor sphere morphology, it can be observed that MV-DNA-CDs@DOX can better deactivate the tumor spheres. This result once again demonstrates that materials with a coating exhibit superior anti-tumor effect.

To investigate the penetration ability of the nanocarrier into solid tumors, a 3D tumor spheroid model was prepared using the hanging drop method. In the experimental design, the nanomaterial was designed to respond to acidic pH and UV light, resulting in particle size reduction, which would facilitate deeper penetration into solid tumors. The tumor spheroids were treated with DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX in an acidic environment (pH 6.5) for 24 h. Confocal scanning (Fig. 5 A) at a depth of 80 μm showed that the red fluorescence of free DOX was distributed on the outer surface of the tumor spheroids and had weaker intensity. In contrast, the red fluorescence of DNA-CDs@DOX and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX was almost evenly distributed throughout the entire tumor spheroid and had significantly stronger intensity compared to the free DOX treatment group. This indicated that the nanocarrier possessed tumor penetration ability. Moreover, after treatment in a neutral environment, the red fluorescence of DOX in DNA-CDs@DOX was weaker than in the acidic environment-treated group. This suggests that the nanomaterial can respond to the tumor microenvironment and enhance the tumor penetration of DOX. When exposed to UV light, the red fluorescence inside the tumor spheroids was enhanced, indicating that DNA-CDs@DOX could simultaneously respond to UV light and enhance the deep penetration of DOX into the tumor (Fig. 5A). Fig. S9 shows the fluorescence semi-quantitative images of the area underlined in Fig. 5A. These results indicate that the nanocarrier could improve the permeability of DOX in tumor spheroids effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and tumor targeting ability
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the pH and UV dual-responsive drug release characteristics of DNA-CDs, it enhances deep penetration into tumors and improves its anti-tumor therapeutic effect. As illustrated in Fig. S10 and S11, in vivo imaging conducted on zebrafish and ex vivo imaging on mouse organs demonstrate that the fluorescent signal of the carrier CDs is suitable for in vivo imaging, but it has limited tissue penetration. Consequently, it is necessary to conjugate the near-infrared dye ICG to facilitate real-time monitoring of the tumor-targeting capabilities of the nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 5B, although free ICG can distribute at the tumor site, the fluorescence intensity of ICG in the MV-DNA-CDs@ICG group in mouse tumors is significantly higher, which may be attributed to the active targeted delivery of ICG by the encapsulated material to the tumor site, resulting in a significant enhancement of ICG fluorescence signal at the tumor site. At 12 h, the MV-DNA-CDs@ICG group still showed strong ICG fluorescence signal, while free ICG and DNA-CDs@ICG group were mostly cleared, indicating that the encapsulated nanomaterials can increase the drug retention time at the tumor site. These results suggest that MV-DNA-CDs can improve drug accumulation and prolong its residence time in tumors. The significant prolongation of residence time at the tumor site can be attributed to two factors, namely, the prolonged circulation and increased accumulation caused by the EPR effect, as well as the slower clearance caused by enhanced penetration in solid tumors.

Mice from different groups were injected with ICG, DNA-CDs@ICG, and MV-DNA-CDs@ICG, and the tumors and major organs were collected after 4 h. Fig. 5C shows that the fluorescence intensity of the MV-DNA-CDs@ICG group is significantly stronger than that of the ICG and DNA-CDs@ICG groups, indicating that the encapsulation significantly enhances the enrichment of ICG at the tumor site. Fig. 5D and Fig. S12 shows that the fluorescence of all groups is concentrated in the liver, lungs, and kidneys. The organ fluorescence in the ICG group is significantly lower than that in the DNA-CDs@ICG and MV-DNA-CDs@ICG groups, which may be due to the faster clearance of ICG in the mouse body. Compared to DNA-CDs@ICG, the liver fluorescence in the MV-DNA-CDs@ICG group is weaker, which may be attributed to the change in the in vivo distribution of the carrier after encapsulation, delivering more ICG to the tumor site, resulting in reduced liver fluorescence. At the same time, this is also beneficial in reducing drug accumulation in the liver and minimizing its impact on liver function. For further study, the fluorescence in Fig. 5B, 5C, and 5D was quantitatively analyzed. As shown in Fig. S13, the encapsulated materials can alter the in vivo distribution of the carrier, delivering more drug to the tumor site and increasing the retention time of the drug at the tumor site.

Fig. 6 A showed the process of animal modeling and treatment. BALB/c mice were subcutaneously implanted with 4 T1 tumors and randomly divided into 7 groups: PBS, DNA-CDs, UV, DOX, DNA-CDs@DOX, MV-DNA-CDs@DOX, and MV-DNA CDs@DOX + UV. Fig. 6B shows the relative tumor volume changes in each group. The relative tumor volume change in the DNA-CDs and UV treatment group was similar to that in the PBS group, indicating that DNA-CDs and UV alone do not have anti-tumor effect. Compared to the DOX group, the DNA-CDs@DOX and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX group exhibited superior tumor inhibition, indicating that the carrier could enhance the anti-tumor effect of the chemotherapeutic drug DOX. After combined UV irradiation, the tumor volume in mice was significantly inhibited, indicating that the carrier could respond to UV light and show optimal therapeutic efficacy. To accurately determine the tumor volume in mice, the tumors were collected and photographed, and the actual tumor size was measured after sacrificing the mice. The tumor growth inhibition effect in Fig. 6C and 6D was similar to the relative tumor volume changes in Fig. 6B. Representative photo of mice on day 0 and day 15 of treatment was shown in Fig. 6F. To assess the cytotoxic effects of different treatment groups on tumor tissues, tumor tissues from each group were collected and stained with H&E and TUNEL (Fig. 6F). In parallel, H&E and TUNEL staining assay demonstrated that the DNA-CDs and DNA-CDs@DOX treatment groups exhibited significantly increased levels of cell apoptosis compared to the PBS control group. These findings indicate that the nanocarrier has a pronounced ability to induce apoptosis and damage tumor cells effectively. It is worth noting that the MV-DNA-CDs@DOX and MV-DNA-CDs@DOX + UV groups had the highest tumor cell necrosis rate, suggesting that the encapsulation enhances tumor targeting and enhances the tumor-killing effect of DOX. In conclusion, all these results indicate that MV-DNA-CDs is an effective carrier for deep penetration of DOX into tumor tissues and enhancing the anti-tumor efficacy of DOX.

To investigate the biocompatibility of MV-DNA-CDs and determine the potential side effects during treatment, the body weight of mice was measured during the treatment period. As shown in Fig. 6E, the results showed that the body weight of mice did not decrease significantly, indicating that the formulation was not toxic to the mice. Further evaluation of in vivo toxicity was performed through H&E staining. Compared to the PBS group, mice showed no significant pathological changes in major organs such as the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, indicating that the formulation had good biocompatibility (Fig. S14A). Liver and kidney function tests showed that the liver and kidney functions of mice in different treatment groups were generally normal (Fig. S14B-E). However, the DOX group showed elevated ALT levels, while the ALT levels in the MV-DNA-CDs@DOX group were within the normal range, indicating that the biomimetic polymer carrier does not affect the health of mice and can reduce the toxicity of DOX (Fig. S14B). These results demonstrate that MV-DNA-CDs have high biocompatibility, and MV-DNA-CDs can be considered a reliable drug carrier.

We developed a secondary dual-responsive and dual-targeting nanocarrier, MV-DNA-CDs, by conjugating carbon dots with biocompatible responsive DNA sequences and encapsulating them with macrophage-derived microvesicles. Various characterization methods confirmed the successful synthesis of the carrier, demonstrating its acid-responsive and light-responsive drug release, as well as its active and passive tumor targeting features. Specifically, DNA-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and can release the model drug, doxorubicin (DOX), in response to mildly acidic tumor environments and lysosomal acidic conditions. Additionally, DNA-CDs can further release DOX upon ultraviolet (UV) light exposure. We used a 3D spheroid tumor model to simulate the complex structure of tumors and assess the antitumor efficacy of the nanocarrier-encapsulated DOX. The results showed that free DOX had significantly reduced cytotoxicity in the 3D spheroid model compared to 2D tumor cells, aligning with clinical observations of DOX's limited efficacy. However, when DOX was loaded into DNA-CDs, its tumor-killing effect in the spheroid model improved significantly. Subsequently, we investigated the mechanisms by which the nanocarrier enhances the tumoricidal effects of doxorubicin (DOX). Permeability experiments indicated that DNA-CDs exhibited better spheroid penetration in acidic environments, and combining UV light irradiation led to even higher tumor penetration. This suggests that the particle size of DNA-CDs decreases in response to acidic tumor conditions and UV light exposure, enabling more DOX to passively target and penetrate deep into the tumor. Thus, we implemented biomimetic modifications to achieve combined active targeting. Using a subcutaneous breast cancer model in mice, we validated the tumor-targeting capability of the MV-DNA-CDs carrier. The results showed that MV-DNA-CDs effectively targeted the tumor site, and the combination treatment of MV-DNA-CDs@DOX with UV light exhibited the most significant antitumor effect.

In summary, we have designed and synthesized MV-DNA-CDs nanocarriers with excellent fluorescent properties, drug-loading capacity, and dual-responsive, dual-targeting drug release features. This system shows great potential as a superior delivery vehicle for anticancer drugs. Moreover, our strategy introduces the use of highly biocompatible DNA as a crosslinker to construct biomimetic organic nanopolymer carriers, providing a novel approach for employing carbon dots as drug carriers and expanding the application of nanotechnology in cancer therapy.
